article.title: NASA Begins Artemis II Countdown as Four Astronauts Prepare for Humanity's First Lunar Voyage in Over 50 Years
article.summary: Countdown clock is running for the April 1 launch of Artemis II, the first crewed mission to the Moon since Apollo 17 in 1972.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's countdown clock began ticking at 4:44 p.m. EDT on March 30, setting the stage for the Artemis II launch no earlier than 6:24 p.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 1, from Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center in Florida. If all goes as planned, four astronauts will ride the Space Launch System rocket and the Orion spacecraft on a 10-day journey around the Moon and back, marking the first time humans have ventured beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 returned in December 1972, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/).

This follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028) on NASA's decision to restructure the Artemis program, which redesignated Artemis III as an orbital test and pushed the first crewed lunar landing to 2028.

## The Crew

The four-person crew includes Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, and Mission Specialist Christina Koch from NASA, alongside Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen of the Canadian Space Agency, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/29/final-preparations-underway-for-nasas-moon-mission/). The crew has been quarantined in the Neil A. Armstrong Operations and Checkout Building, following controlled sleep schedules and nutrition plans while completing medical verifications, as reported by [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/).

The mission carries several historic firsts. Victor Glover will become the first Black astronaut to travel beyond low Earth orbit, Christina Koch will be the first woman to fly around the Moon, and Jeremy Hansen will be the first Canadian and first non-American to make the journey, according to [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/science/space/artemis-ii-astronauts-names-who-are-nasa-moon-mission-rcna265105). "Twenty-four men have seen the moon, and we're going to send the first set of woman's eyes," Glover said, as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## The Flight Plan

The mission begins with an eight-minute climb to space aboard the SLS rocket, which generates nearly 9 million pounds of thrust at liftoff with a combined vehicle weight of 5.7 million pounds, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). After reaching low Earth orbit, the crew will perform two rocket firings over the first 90 minutes, followed by separation from the upper stage roughly three hours and 23 minutes after launch.

The crew will spend approximately 24 hours orbiting Earth to test critical life support systems aboard the Orion capsule, including carbon dioxide scrubbing, water systems, and other crew sustainability hardware making their first crewed flight, as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). About 25 hours after launch, a six-minute, five-second trans-lunar injection burn will boost the spacecraft's velocity by roughly 900 mph and send the crew on a four-day coast to the Moon.

At closest approach, Artemis II will pass within 4,100 miles of the lunar surface, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). The spacecraft is expected to reach a maximum distance of approximately 252,000 miles from Earth, surpassing the record set by Apollo 13 of 248,655 miles. "There are actually places on the far side that have never been seen by human eyes," Koch noted, as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

Unlike the Apollo 8 mission in 1968, Artemis II will not enter lunar orbit. Instead, the spacecraft follows a free return trajectory that uses the Moon's gravity to curve the flight path back toward Earth, a design that provides an automatic return even if major propulsion systems fail after lunar injection, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## Reentry and Recovery

Splashdown is targeted for Friday, April 10, in the Pacific Ocean. The Orion capsule will reenter the atmosphere at approximately 25,000 mph, with heat shield temperatures reaching up to 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit, followed by a roughly five-minute radio blackout before parachute deployment, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). The 16.5-foot-wide heat shield incorporates lessons learned from Artemis I in 2022, which experienced unexpected damage during its skip reentry trajectory. The revised entry pattern for Artemis II allows more even erosion of the heat shield's char layer, as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## What We Don't Know

While weather conditions currently show an 80% chance of favorable launch weather, cloud coverage and high winds on the ground remain primary concerns that teams will monitor through launch day, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/weather-for-nasas-artemis-ii-mission-launch-80-favorable/). The two-hour launch window provides some flexibility, but conditions must remain within acceptable parameters.

The heat shield performance remains an open question. Artemis I revealed that the ablative material behaved differently than models predicted, and engineers have adjusted the reentry profile accordingly, but the actual thermal protection performance under crewed conditions will only be validated during this flight, as noted by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

Commander Wiseman has been direct about the test-flight nature of the mission. "This is a test mission. When we get off the planet, we might come right back home... we are ready for every scenario," he said, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). Hansen was equally candid about the risks: "You can lose a crew. And if we do, that shouldn't shock us. And the most important thing we do next is we stack the next rocket," as reported by [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

Live coverage of tanking operations begins at 7:45 a.m. EDT on April 1 on NASA's YouTube channel, with full mission coverage starting at 12:50 p.m., according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/30/nasas-artemis-ii-launch-mission-countdown-begins/).
